name: loop-rate-limiters

metadata.name: loop-rate-limiters
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License

documentation:
# Loop rate limiters

[![Build](https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/upkie/loop-rate-limiters/main.yml?branch=main)](https://github.com/upkie/loop-rate-limiters/actions)
[![Documentation](https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/upkie/loop-rate-limiters/docs.yml?branch=main&label=docs)](https://upkie.github.io/loop-rate-limiters/)
[![Coverage](https://coveralls.io/repos/github/upkie/loop-rate-limiters/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://coveralls.io/github/upkie/loop-rate-limiters?branch=main)
[![Conda version](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/loop-rate-limiters/badges/version.svg)](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/loop-rate-limiters)
[![PyPI version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/loop-rate-limiters)](https://pypi.org/project/loop-rate-limiters/)

Simple loop frequency regulators in Python with an API similar to ``rospy.Rate``:

```python
from loop_rate_limiters import RateLimiter
from time import perf_counter

rate = RateLimiter(frequency=400.0)
while True:
    print(f"Hello from loop at {perf_counter():.3f} s")
    rate.sleep()
```

A similar ``AsyncRateLimiter`` class is available for [asynchronous code](https://github.com/upkie/loop-rate-limiters#asynchronous-io).

## Installation

You can install the library straight from PyPI:

```console
$ pip install loop-rate-limiters
```

Or equivalently from Conda:

```console
$ conda install -c conda-forge loop-rate-limiters
```

## Asynchronous I/O

The ``AsyncRateLimiter`` class provides a loop frequency limiter for [asyncio](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio.html):

```python
import asyncio
from loop_rate_limiters import AsyncRateLimiter

async def main():
    rate = AsyncRateLimiter(frequency=400.0)
    while True:
        loop_time = asyncio.get_event_loop().time()
        print(f"Hello from loop at {loop_time:.3f} s")
        await rate.sleep()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
```

methods.name: loop_rate_limiters.rate_limiter.RateLimiter.__init__
documentation:
Initialize rate limiter.

Args:
    frequency: Desired frequency in hertz.
    name: Human-readable name used for logging.
    warn: If set (default), warn when the time between two calls
        exceeded the rate clock.

methods.name: loop_rate_limiters.rate_limiter.RateLimiter.dt
documentation: Desired period between two calls to {func}`sleep`, in seconds.

methods.name: loop_rate_limiters.rate_limiter.RateLimiter.next_tick
documentation: Time of next clock tick.

methods.name: loop_rate_limiters.rate_limiter.RateLimiter.period
documentation: Desired period between two calls to {func}`sleep`, in seconds.

methods.name: loop_rate_limiters.rate_limiter.RateLimiter.slack
documentation:
Slack duration computed at the last call to {func}`sleep`.

This duration is in seconds.

methods.name: loop_rate_limiters.rate_limiter.RateLimiter.remaining
documentation:
Get the time remaining until the next expected clock tick.

Returns:
    Time remaining, in seconds, until the next expected clock tick.

methods.name: loop_rate_limiters.rate_limiter.RateLimiter.sleep
documentation: Sleep for the duration required to regulate inter-call frequency.

Regulate the frequency between calls to the same instruction.

This rate limniter is meant to be used in e.g. a loop or callback function.
It is, in essence, the same as [rospy.Rate]. It assumes Python's performance
counter never jumps backward nor forward, so that it does not handle such
cases contrary to [rospy.Rate].

Attributes:

: name: Human-readable name used for logging.
  warn: If set (default), warn when the time between two calls

  > exceeded the rate clock.

[rospy.rate]: https://github.com/ros/ros_comm/blob/noetic-devel/clients/rospy/src/rospy/timer.py

modules.name: loop_rate_limiters.async_rate_limiter
documentation:
Non-blocking loop frequency limiter for asyncio.

Note that there is a difference between a (non-blocking) rate limiter and a
(blocking) synchronous clock, which lies in the behavior when skipping cycles.
A rate limiter does nothing if there is no time left, as the caller's rate does
not need to be limited. On the contrary, a synchronous clock waits for the next
tick, which is by definition in the future, so it always waits for a non-zero
duration.

methods.name: loop_rate_limiters.async_rate_limiter.AsyncRateLimiter.sleep
documentation:
Sleep the duration required to regulate the loop frequency.

This function is meant to be called once per loop cycle.

Args:
: block_duration: the coroutine blocks the event loop for this

  : duration (in seconds) before the next tick. It is non-blocking
    before that.

Note:

: A call to this function will be non-blocking *except* for the last
  `block_duration` seconds of the limiter period.

The block duration helps trim period overshoots and brings the measured
period much closer to the desired one (\< 2% average error vs. 8-12%
average error with a single asyncio.sleep). Empirically a block
duration of 0.5 ms gives good behavior at 400 Hz or lower.

Loop frequency regulator.

Calls to {func}`sleep` are non-blocking most of the time but become
blocking close to the next clock tick to get more reliable loop
frequencies.

This rate limiter is in essence the same as in the one from [pymanoid]. It
relies on the event loop time never jumping backwards nor forwards, so that
it does not handle such cases contrary to e.g. [rospy.Rate].

Attributes:

: name: Human-readable name used for logging.
  warn: If set (default), warn when the time between two calls

  > exceeded the rate clock.

[pymanoid]: https://github.com/stephane-caron/pymanoid/blob/d3e2098e40656943f2639f90a1ec4269cf730157/pymanoid/sim.py#L140
[rospy.rate]: https://github.com/ros/ros_comm/blob/noetic-devel/clients/rospy/src/rospy/timer.py
